schools.summary: The measures (seah), prices, and 'the gate' as the corn market are fixed; the chapter division is faulted for breaking a single story.
Clarke and Wesley reckon the seah and shekel to show how vast a reversal the cheap corn was.
Henry: man's extremity is God's opportunity; the word came when the king had given up waiting.
Gill: Elisha spoke the promise to the king and those with him.
The shalish is the king's adjutant; his 'windows in heaven' is scoffing unbelief, and Elisha's reply mimics his own words.
Clarke: the doom is a mere prediction of death, not a judgment for unbelief; any man might have said as much.
Henry: the death is the just doom of infidelity, like the murmurers who saw Canaan but could not enter.
Gill: bold unbelief in an honoured man, rebuked at the very word that would have fed him.

debates.question: Is the captain's foretold death a judgment for his unbelief or a natural consequence?

sides.position: a signed judgment upon distrust of God's word
a mere prediction of death, the ordinary hazard of a crowded gate

schools.summary: The lepers sit outside the gate by the law of exclusion (Lev. 13; Num. 5), separated from human society.
Gill: the law kept them outside, yet they clung near the gate for relief and safety.
Clarke: they were not permitted to mingle in civil society; the Gemara names them Gehazi and his sons.
Henry: Samaria was delivered and did not know it; Providence made the outcasts its messengers.

outliers.note: The Gemara, Rashi, and Jarchi identify the four lepers as Gehazi and his three sons.

schools.summary: The lepers' three-way logic: death in the city, death at the gate, only hope in going over to the Syrians ('fall unto' = desert).
Gill: to enter the city would break the law that barred them, closing even that option.
Henry: common prudence ventures on mercy rather than sitting still to starve, as the prodigal returned; 'we shall but die' becomes the language of one safe from the second death.
'In the twilight' is nightfall, chosen so their going was unseen; the 'uttermost part' is the camp's edge nearest Samaria, where the outpost stood empty.
Gill: the dusk of the evening; the men brace for an enemy and find none.
Henry: Providence timed it exactly — the Syrians fled in the twilight and in the twilight the lepers came, so no time was lost.
The 'noise' (a word used of thunder) is likely a sound in the air among the hills; the Hittites were a real northern power, the Egyptian plural rhetorical.
Henry: not by might but by the Spirit; as God imposed on sight at Dothan, here he imposes on hearing — fear where no fear was.
Gill: the noise came by angels, or by imagination, or directly in their ears.
Wesley: 'Hittites' stands loosely for the surviving peoples of Canaan; the Egyptian plural means the one king or his governors.

debates.question: Who are the 'kings of the Hittites' the Syrians dreaded?

sides.position: the historic Hittite empire between the Euphrates and Orontes
the scattered remnant of the Canaanite peoples

schools.summary: Ellicott: a vivid picture of wild flight — tents, horses, and asses abandoned, the camp left 'as it was.'
Wesley: no scouts, no courage to face the phantom foe; 'the wicked flee when none pursueth.'
Henry: God can dispirit the boldest and make the stoutest heart tremble at the shaking of a leaf.
Gill: they fled in the dark, and the lepers came upon the camp very soon after.

themes: the wicked flee when none pursue

schools.summary: The narrative resumes from v5: the lepers eat, then carry off silver, gold, and raiment and hide it, as Orientals hid treasure in holes for want of banks.
'A day of good tidings' must not be kept silent; 'some mischief' is literally 'guilt will find us' — blame for concealing the news.
Gill: it is not right to keep the booty to themselves; it wrongs their brethren and may not end well.
Henry: they who have found the unsearchable riches of Christ will not delay to report the good tidings; selfishness cannot prosper.
'Porter' is likely collective; the report is exact — no man, not a human voice, horses and asses tethered, tents as they were.
Gill: they called to the chief of the gatekeepers, of whom there was more than one.
The verb is singular and perhaps impersonal ('one called, the warders'); 'within' suggests the palace stood near the ramparts.
Gill: the city porter called the palace porters, who told the king's household.
Henry: the news was not the less acceptable for being first brought by lepers.

themes: the report ascends to the palace

schools.summary: The king suspects a ruse — the Syrians feigning flight to draw the city out; 'misery is suspicious,' and such stratagems are common in war.
Clarke: the king had made a noble defence and shared the siege's sufferings — even in vile Ahab there were some good things.
Henry: a guilty conscience fears the worst; having forfeited God's favour by unbelief, the king cannot credit the deliverance.

debates.question: Is the king's suspicion prudent generalship or unbelieving dread?

sides.position: sober caution against a common military stratagem
the restless fear of a guilty, unbelieving conscience

schools.summary: 'Five' is an indefinite small number; the servant advises sending scouts on the surviving horses to learn the Syrians' fate.
Wesley reads the Hebrew particle as certainty, not likeness — the horses are all that famine has left of Israel's cavalry.
Gill: the horses that had not died in the dearth.

debates.question: How should the doubled, difficult clause about the horses be handled?

sides.position: a copyist's dittography to be deleted (following Kennicott)
a genuine pathetic emphasis on the few survivors, to be kept
an affirmation of certainty, to be re-pointed and re-read

schools.summary: Two teams (chariots of horses) are sent, the best-fed beasts; two so that if one is captured the other may escape with the news.
Gill: only two, perhaps the king's own chariot horses, as fittest for the errand.
Clarke: five were intended, but only two proved effective.
The road toward Jordan is strewn with garments and vessels cast away 'in their haste' — the very signature of a headlong flight.
Gill: the scouts followed the homeward route as far as Jordan, the river the Syrians must cross.
Clarke: the scattered garments are a manifest proof of the hurry and precipitancy of the flight.
The people spoil the camp, and provisions become cheap exactly as foretold — 'according to the word of the Lord.'
Gill: such plenty that there was enough not only for use but for sale, fulfilling the price Elisha named.
Henry: the wealth of the sinner laid up for the just, the spoilers spoiled; no word of God shall fall to the ground.
The lord is set over the gate to keep order and is trodden to death by the crowd — a literal fulfilment that won the prophet great credit.
Gill: he was appointed not to keep out an enemy but to marshal the people streaming out for spoil.
Henry: he saw the plenty to shame his unbelief but did not eat of it — judgment came between the cup and the lip.

themes: saw the plenty but did not taste it

schools.summary: The narrator restates the promise verbatim beside its fulfilment, so prediction and event may be exactly compared.
Henry: the matter is repeated that we might take special notice — not one word of God shall fall to the ground.
The scoffer's 'windows in heaven' is requoted; he could imagine plenty only by a second deluge and so limited God to his own measure.
Henry: the verse shows how deeply God resents distrust of his power and promise — 'Can God furnish a table?' provoked his wrath.
The third recital matches the prediction to the event syllable for syllable, sealing the prophet's word.
Clarke: such literal fulfilments won the prophet great credit in Israel.
Henry: honour cannot secure from sudden death — he whom the king leaned on the people trod on; God's threatenings are as sure as his promises.

themes: unbelief shut out from the plenty
